summary: DiagnosticsStateTest delivers a clean 1:1 mapping to the 7 TDD anchors from task 4. Each test exercises the real DiagnosticsState (no mocks, pure-JVM JUnit4 per patterns.md), uses full data-class equality assertions on InitSnapshot, and the concurrent test is structurally sound: 10 000 iterations, synchronous start via CountDownLatch(1), both threads joined via CountDownLatch(2) with a 30s timeout, reader-thread AssertionError propagated to the test thread via AtomicReference. Naming and structure mirror the AppCorruptionStateTest reference pattern. No critical or major issues.

findings.issue: In concurrentWriterReaderNeverSeesMixedState the reader treats `lastInitSnapshot() == null` as a silent skip (`return@repeat`). Over 10 000 iterations on a fast machine the writer normally starts publishing immediately, but the test does not record how many iterations actually observed a non-null snapshot. If the writer thread stalled (or scheduling pinned writer after reader completed), the reader could finish all 10 000 iterations seeing only null and the test would pass without exercising any race window — a silent loss of coverage rather than a correctness bug.
findings.recommendation: Track an `observedNonNull = AtomicInteger(0)` in the reader: increment on each non-null read, then after both threads join assert `assertTrue("reader never observed a published snapshot — race window was empty", observedNonNull.get() > 0)`. This guarantees the test actually witnessed concurrent state in addition to validating the no-mixed-state invariant. No mock changes required.

findings.issue: No test pins the contract for repeated `onInitEnd` after a terminal outcome (e.g. `onInitStart` -> `onInitEnd(true)` -> `onInitEnd(false)`). Per Decision 7, `updateAndGet { current?.copy(outcome = ...) }` will happily flip `Ok` to `Failed` on a second call. Task 6 says `lifecycleMutex` makes this scenario impossible in production, but the existing `secondOnInitStartReplacesPreviousAttempt` test fixes the analogous replace-semantics for `onInitStart`, so leaving the symmetric `onInitEnd` behaviour undocumented is an asymmetric gap. This is not in the TDD anchor list, so it is genuinely optional, but a one-line test would lock in the contract.
findings.recommendation: Add a single test, e.g. `secondOnInitEndOverwritesOutcome`: `state.onInitStart("model-x", FREE_RAM_A, AT_A); state.onInitEnd(true); state.onInitEnd(false); assertEquals(InitSnapshot("model-x", FREE_RAM_A, AT_A, Outcome.Failed), state.lastInitSnapshot())`. If the team prefers strictly TDD-anchor-bound test files, keep the gap and note it in tech-spec § Edge cases instead.
